clinical_history: ECG from a young adult man. What is the most likely diagnosis?
diagnosis: The ECG shows borderline sinus tachycardia at rest with a prolonged PR interval (first degree AV block) at 220 ms. Evidence for prominent left atrial abnormality (LAA) with probable actual enlargement/dilation is present. A left ventricular (LV) intraventricular conduction delay (IVCD) is present (wide QRS at 140 ms), with a leftward QRS axis (about -30 degrees). Notching and slurring of the widened QRS suggest underlying ventricular scarring/fibrosis. The combination of the LAA, prominent precordial voltage and LV-IVCD suggests underlying left ventricular hypertrophy. R wave progression (V1-V3) is very slow, a non-diagnostic finding in the presence of the LV-IVCD, as is the slight J/ST elevation in leads V1-V3. Recent transthoracic echocardiograms showed marked LV cavity dilation with global, severe hypokinesis. The clinical diagnosis was idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy.
